sentences.grammarNote: مَرحَباً (marhaban) = hello/welcome — an accusative verbal noun used as a greeting, from root ر-ح-ب (r-ḥ-b, to be spacious/welcoming). أنا جيف (ana Jeff) is a nominal sentence with no verb — 'I Jeff' = 'I am Jeff.' Arabic drops the verb كون (to be) in the present tense, so 'I am X' is simply أنا + X.

هَذَا (hādhā) = this (masculine demonstrative pronoun). مَوقِعِي (mawqiʿī, my website) — مَوقِع from root و-ق-ع (to fall/be located): مَوقِع = position/site, a مَفعِل pattern place-noun. The suffix ي (ī) = my. الإِلِكتُرُونِي (al-iliktrūnī, the electronic) is a definite adjective agreeing with the definite possessed noun مَوقِعِي.

sentences.base_language: I like learning languages but it is very hard.
sentences.target_language: أحب تعلم اللغات لكنه صعب جداً.
sentences.romanized: uhibb ta'allum al-lughat lakinahu sa'b jiddan.
sentences.grammarNote: لكنه (lakinahu) = لكن + ه — 'but it'. The suffix ه (hu) refers back to التعلم (learning). Attaching pronouns as suffixes is common in Arabic.

sentences.base_language: I am not very good at speaking Arabic yet.
sentences.target_language: لا أتحدث العربية بشكل جيد بعد.
sentences.romanized: la ataḥaddath al-arabiyya bi-shakl jayyid ba'd.
sentences.literal: Not I-speak Arabic in-manner good yet.
sentences.grammarNote: أَتَحَدَّث (ataḥaddath, I speak) — Form V of root ح-د-ث (ḥ-d-th, to occur/tell): تَحَدَّثَ = to speak/converse (reflexive of Form II حَدَّثَ = to tell). بِشَكل جَيِّد (bi-shakl jayyid, in a good manner/well) is a common adverbial phrase: بِشَكل + adjective. بَعد (baʿd, yet/still) at the end of a negated sentence means 'yet.'

sentences.base_language: I learned with other apps and they were great too.
sentences.target_language: تعلمت مع تطبيقات أخرى وكانت رائعة أيضاً.
sentences.romanized: ta'allamtu ma'a tatbiqat ukhra wa-kanat ra'i'a aydan.
sentences.literal: I-learned with applications other and-were wonderful also.
sentences.grammarNote: تَعَلَّمتُ (taʿallamtu, I learned) — Form V of root ع-ل-م (ʿ-l-m, to know): Form V تَعَلَّمَ = to learn (reflexive of Form II عَلَّمَ = to teach). تَطبِيقَات (taṭbīqāt, apps) — sound feminine plural of تَطبِيق (application, Form II verbal noun from ط-ب-ق, to apply). كَانَت رَائِعَة (kānat rāʾiʿa) — كان in past tense acts as a linking verb: 'they were wonderful.'

sentences.base_language: But I made this app to do more immersion.
sentences.target_language: لكنني صنعت هذا التطبيق للقيام بالانغماس أكثر.
sentences.romanized: lakinani sana'tu hadha at-tatbiq lil-qiyam bi-l-inghimas akthar.
sentences.grammarNote: للقيام بـ (lil-qiyam bi-) = 'for doing / in order to do'. لـ + ال + قيام + بـ — this construction is common for expressing purpose.

يُمكِنُكَ أَن (yumkinuka an, it is possible for you to / you can) — an impersonal construction: يُمكِن (it is possible) + accusative pronoun suffix كَ (you). This is the standard MSA way to say 'you can do X': يُمكِنُكَ أَن + present-tense verb. مَعِي (maʿī, with me) — مَع (with) + pronoun suffix ي.

sentences.base_language: I tried to make this easy to use but I may have made mistakes.
sentences.target_language: حاولت جعله سهل الاستخدام لكن ربما أخطأت.
sentences.romanized: hawaltu ja'lahu sahl al-istikhdam lakin rubbama akhta'tu.
sentences.literal: I-tried making-it easy the-use but maybe I-made-mistakes.
sentences.grammarNote: حَاوَلتُ (ḥāwaltu, I tried) — Form III of root ح-و-ل (ḥ-w-l): Form III حَاوَلَ = to attempt/try (implies effort toward a goal). جَعلَهُ (jaʿlahu, making it) — verbal noun جَعل (making/causing) + suffix هُ (it). رُبَّمَا (rubbamā, perhaps/maybe) is a particle of doubt, followed by a verb in the past or present.

sentences.base_language: If you want to help me, that would be great.
sentences.target_language: إذا أردت مساعدتي، سيكون ذلك رائعاً.
sentences.romanized: idha aradta musa'adati, sa-yakun dhalika ra'i'an.
sentences.grammarNote: إذا (idha) = if, used for future conditions. سيكون (sa-yakun) = will be (سـ prefix + يكون).

sentences.base_language: If you like this website, please share it.
sentences.target_language: إذا أعجبك هذا الموقع، من فضلك شاركه.
sentences.romanized: idha a'jabaka hadha al-mawqi', min fadlak sharikhu.
sentences.literal: If pleased-you this the-site, from your-favor share-it.
sentences.grammarNote: أَعجَبَكَ (aʿjabaka, it pleased you) — Form IV of root ع-ج-ب (ʿ-j-b, to be amazed): أَعجَبَ = to please/delight. The subject is هذا الموقع and the object is كَ (you). Arabic expresses 'you like' as 'it pleases you.' شَارِكهُ (shārikhu, share it!) — Form III imperative of ش-ر-ك (to share/participate) + object suffix هُ (it).

sentences.base_language: If you know other languages, you can make lessons!
sentences.target_language: إذا كنت تعرف لغات أخرى، يمكنك إنشاء دروس!
sentences.romanized: idha kunta ta'rif lughat ukhra, yumkinuka insha' durus!
sentences.literal: If you-were you-know languages other, possible-for-you creating lessons!
sentences.grammarNote: لُغَات أُخرى (lughāt ukhrā, other languages) — لُغَات is the sound feminine plural of لُغَة (language). أُخرى (ukhrā, other — feminine) is the feminine of آخَر (ākhar, other). إِنشَاء (inshāʾ, creating/establishing) — Form IV verbal noun of ن-ش-أ (to originate/create). دُرُوس (durūs, lessons) — broken plural of دَرس (dars), pattern فُعُول.

sentences.base_language: If you can't help, that is OK.
sentences.target_language: إذا لم تستطع المساعدة، فلا بأس.
sentences.romanized: idha lam tastati' al-musa'ada, fala ba's.
sentences.literal: If not you-were-able the-help, then-no harm.
sentences.grammarNote: لَم تَستَطِع (lam tastatiʿ, you were not able) — لَم negates the past using a jussive verb form: the final vowel is dropped. اِستَطَاعَ (istaṭāʿa, to be able) — Form X of ط-و-ع (to obey/be flexible): the root concept of capability. فَلَا بَأس (falā baʾs, then no harm/that's OK) — a reassuring phrase: لَا بَأس = no harm, no problem.

sentences.base_language: I am happy you are learning with me either way.
sentences.target_language: أنا سعيد لأنك تتعلم معي في كلتا الحالتين.
sentences.romanized: ana sa'id li'annaka tata'allam ma'i fi kilta al-halatayn.
sentences.literal: I happy because-you you-learn with-me in both the-two-cases.
sentences.grammarNote: كِلتَا الحَالَتَين (kiltā al-ḥālatayn, both cases) — كِلتَا is the feminine dual of كِلَا (kilā, both). الحَالَتَين is the dual of الحَالَة (case/situation). The dual suffix ـَتَين (-atayn) marks two feminine nouns. لِأَنَّكَ (liʾannaka, because you) — لِأَنَّ (because) + suffix كَ (you m.): a subordinating conjunction introducing a reason clause.
